data.title: UTG: Still A Solid Long-Term Holding, But Some Caution To Consider
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Reaves Utility Income Trust (NYSE:UTG) is a closed-end fund providing exposure to primarily U.S. utilities. The fund is one of the few that can boast that they have never cut their regular monthly distribution to investors. That's even more impressive when a fund has an inception date going back to early 2004, leading to two decades of consistent income for its investors.

Since our last update, UTG has moved mostly sideways but has still provided some total returns to investors when factoring in the distributions.

That said, that was after making a massive run higher on the back of some hot utility names that are set to benefit in powering the AI revolution. So, seeing some sideways movement or a pause after a run-up isn't necessarily unexpected. I believe that UTG is still worth investing in for the long term. However, the lack of a meaningful discount for this CEF and some of the underlying utility holdings getting into richly priced territory on the back of AI hype are areas of concern. More patient investors likely wouldn't be buying this name, or at least not aggressively buying.

UTG Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.36
  • Discount/Premium: 0.03%
  • Distribution Yield: 6.92%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.93%
  • Leverage: 18.37%
  • Managed Assets: $3.539 billion
  • Structure: Perpetual

UTG's investment objective is "to provide a high level of after-tax total return, consisting primarily of tax-advantaged dividend income and capital appreciation." To achieve this, the fund "intends to invest at least 80% of its total assets in dividend-paying common and preferred stocks and debt instruments of companies within the utility industry."

Performance Comparison

With the strong returns UTG has been delivering, it has now surpassed its peer fund, the Cohen & Steers Infrastructure Fund (UTF). This also includes UTG now outperforming Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLU) over this last three-year period.

UTF is a common comparison, even if it has some slight differences because both are relatively older funds compared to the others in the infrastructure space. They are considered the higher-quality CEF names. One boast that UTG has that UTF can't claim is never cutting its regular distribution, as they cut during the Global Financial Crisis. At this point, UTF has still never reclaimed the highwater mark set pre-GFC when it was paying $0.2075 per month. However, the NAV is higher than at its inception, meaning it would appear they have chosen to try to keep the NAV stable or growing.

When looking back further in regard to total return comparisons to XLU, we can see that UTG has underperformed over the last decade. That also includes UTF, which lagged against the passively managed index ETF as well.

Where we see that UTG and UTF had outperformed is on a total return basis. That is, the share price performance that an investor could pick up these funds at 10 years ago was at a discount to the NAV per share. Both of these funds experienced discount narrowing and have now regularly flirted with trading at premiums for several years.

This is why I would temper expectations for wanting to pick up shares of UTG (or even UTF) too aggressively. They were able to outperform historically if an investor could scoop them up at a decent discount, but at premium prices, it becomes more difficult to outperform something like XLU on a total return basis. That said, some investors only focus more on the distributions being paid—and on that front, UTG and UTF deliver a distribution rate meaningfully higher than XLU.

Distribution - Steady

Given that focus for some investors on the distribution instead, UTG has been able to deliver consistently over the years. As we noted, boasting that they have never cut their regular distribution since its inception in early 2004.

To cover this distribution, the fund will rely on capital gains, as net investment income coverage came to just 21.8% in the last fiscal year. The fund also writes covered calls, but this hasn't been a significant driver of providing capital gains for the fund.

Given that the fund is trading at a premium regularly, they also benefit from growing assets in an accretive manner through its DRIP and at-the-market offerings. The ATM is a significant source of seeing net assets increase and contributed to just over $281 million in fiscal 2024. As the fund issues more shares, it's only natural to see NII increase when looking at the raw figure.

That's where looking at NII per share can be more helpful. NII per share has decreased from $0.55 to $0.49 in the last year, despite the fact that the company's underlying holdings have generally seen dividend increases. They noted that there were 47 increases in their underlying holdings, and the average increase was 6.2%. Total investment income increased to $96.1 million, up from $88.7 million seen in fiscal 2023. The fund's total expenses rose to $55.365 million from $47.798 million. That was good enough to wipe out the increase in the TII and contributed to the decrease in NII per share we would have seen.

Further, while issuing new shares is accretive to NAV, that isn't always the case with income generation. The NAV distribution rate of the fund comes to 6.93%, but most of the underlying portfolio they invest in don't pay that high of rates. That means NII from year to year can be the same as the increase in both TII and expenses negate each other, but there are now more shares issued. Therefore, the NII per share will take a hit, and that's why we've seen a decrease over the last year.

One benefit the fund could see would be the Fed continuing to cut rates. This is because of the fund's leverage, though it is fairly modest, it still comes with a cost. When including the fund's leverage expenses, the total expense ratio comes to 2.43%. Given their borrowings are based on SOFR plus 0.65%, what the Fed does matters. As their fiscal year-end comes in October, this report doesn't reflect the 100 basis points of cuts the Fed has put through.

That said, the stats we do see are that the weighted average rate during the fiscal year was 5.93%, which came down to 5.35% at the end of the reporting period. From there, it would have been further reduced by another roughly 50 basis points. All else being equal, that should help keep NII per share flat, even if they are issuing more shares.

In looking at the tax classifications of the distribution for UTG, they provide a handy chart. As it is consistent with what we would expect, long-term capital gains are a significant source of covering the distribution to investors. The other source that is most highly represented is the qualified dividends.

Both qualified dividends and long-term capital gains are tax-friendly relative to ordinary income, which can make this fund appropriate for being held in a taxable account. There can be periods of return of capital, which is also beneficial in a taxable account because it defers the tax obligation by reducing an investor's cost basis. As long as the NAV is stable or rising over time, then this isn't a concern either.

UTG's Portfolio

The fund is primarily invested in utilities by a significant margin. However, there is still a fair bit allocated to communication services, energy, industrials, and real estate. This is fairly consistent with what we saw in our prior update. Communication services has seen the weight slightly lowered, while energy has seen its sleeves slightly expand.

When looking at the top ten, I'd first note the fund is rather highly concentrated. In total, at the end of the calendar year 2024, they listed 58 total holdings. That isn't too many compared to some other CEFs that hold a hundred or even more, which includes UTF. The top ten then dominate nearly 50% of UTG's portfolio.

A few of those names really stand out, too, as they have made almost parabolic moves over the last couple of years due to AI-driven expectations. These include Talen Energy (TLN), Vistra (VST) and Constellation Energy (CEG).

These sorts of moves higher are rather unusual for utility names, which are often stable, boring, cash-flowing machines.

To get a sense of valuations here, we can look at TLN with a forward P/E of nearly 45x, where the sector median is 17.5x. At the same time, they are also expected to see their earnings absolutely explode higher. If these analysts' estimates are achieved, then TLN isn't looking too richly priced.

VST and CEG aren't as richly priced relative to TLN, but their earnings are also not expected to see such extreme growth. If these lofty expectations moving forward aren't achieved, then we could see a sharp drop in these names. We also see that they've become incredibly volatile as they shift from the usual boring utility type of investment that we are mostly familiar with into growth utilities.

Conclusion

UTG is a solid long-term closed-end fund that has benefited from its active management, which invested capital in the right names. However, those names are richly priced and have lofty expectations. If these expectations aren't met, we could see these holdings drop sharply, which would take UTG with it. Basically, reversing the sharp gains that we saw over the last year that contributed outsized gains to UTG in the first place.

Additionally, UTG also has continued to flirt with premium pricing itself. That can be beneficial for issuing new shares accretively, but it also puts the fund in a situation where discount widening could negatively impact the fund. As we saw over the last decade, that contributed to the fund's overall total returns, it could be the opposite if we get into some volatility.

Given those two factors, there is certainly some caution that seems warranted. That said, I believe that UTG is worth investing in for the long run and has a real shot at continuing to be a reliable monthly payer. I just wouldn't necessarily be adding aggressively. As we have been seeing more volatility in this name, I wouldn't be surprised to see more of that volatility potentially remaining going forward.

data.title: Copa Holdings: Solid Business Model Continues To Deliver
data.description:
Copa Holdings/Copa Airlines (NYSE:CPA) has a unique business model that continues to inspire other carriers. For those not familiar with the company, Copa explores its singular position in Panamá to connect South America to Central and North America using a fleet consisting of only Boeing 737s. The quality of its strategy and execution is underpinned by the fact that the company is significantly profitable, usually delivering mid-teens Net Income, and didn't issue any shares during Covid in order to survive 2020.

Copa currently sports Strong Buy from both SA Analysts and Wall Street, while a Hold from Quant. As per its Factor Grades, Valuation is A+ due to Forward Price to Earnings at 6x or below in future years, while Profitability is A due to the already mentioned mid-teens Net Income. Growth took a downgrade to C after the earnings call because Copa didn't grow Revenue YoY mainly due to the MAX 9 partial grounding in January, suspension of flights to Venezuela in July and weak currency in several Latin American countries during the second half of 2024.

In this article, I'll start by discussing Copa's FY'24 earnings results and why I think the company will surprise to the upside during FY'25, followed by a Valuation and Risks section. I believe Copa Airlines is a Buy due to its impressive Net Income supported by a resilient business model and low valuation.

Despite Weak Revenue Growth, FY'24 Earnings Were Solid

Copa Airlines reported its FY'24 earnings on February 13th, highlighting its increase in Available Seat Miles (ASM) of 8.6% and an almost stable Load Factor of 86.3% compared to 86.8% in FY'23. However, this was offset by a decrease in Revenue per Available Seat Miles (RASM) of -8.2%, which brought Total Revenue lower by 0.4% YoY. Since Q4'24 was its worst in terms of Revenue Growth at -3.9%, I believe the weak currency environment following the US election is the major culprit for this flat performance. But good news are on the horizon, as these currencies have since recovered after Donald Trump's inauguration: Since January, against USD, the Colombian Peso and Chilean Peso are up by ~6% and the Brazilian Real is up by ~8%. This means Copa Airlines will make more money without raising prices for those consumers, just recovering what it lost during Q4 in FX translation.

The graph below highlights the strength of Copa Airlines' business model. The two weak years prior to FY'20 have a good reason: FY'15 was impacted by an FX accounting due to Venezuelan currency devaluation of ~$458M and FY'18 due to an Asset Write-down of ~$188M, so adjusted Net Income for those years is $233M in FY'15 and $276M in FY'18, in line with historic data.

Note how quickly the company recovered and surpassed its historic Net Income after FY'20, even with a slowdown in Revenue during FY'24. Even though it earns almost double of pre-Covid, its $4B Market Cap is still quite significantly below the $5.8B from FY'18. As part of its Q4'24 Earnings, Management has issued a 2025 Outlook targeting 7 to 8% growth in ASM and 20 to 22% Operating Margin. Both are in line with what it delivered in 2024.

I have two reasons to believe that this guidance can be easily surpassed. First, the company achieved its 2024 ASM growth while adding only six aircraft to its fleet (2023-F, Page 33) and facing a partial, albeit short, MAX 9 grounding back in January. For FY'25, it is targeting an additional fourteen aircraft to be received. The risk here is that all issues plaguing Boeing in recent years will continue during FY'25 and force Copa to change its Fleet Plan again, but positive news have arrived at the end of January with Boeing delivering 45 jets - highest since December 2023. So for now, this incremental fourteen aircraft seems to be on track.

The second reason is what they don't say in this Outlook in terms of Revenue. By sharing only ASM capacity, it lets RASM up for guessing. Even though the currency scenario has improved, it will still face a tough comparison against the H1'24, so I'm expecting a negative RASM in Q1 and Q2. However, I do expect this trend to improve as price increases have been rolled out, and the FX comparison will ease or be favorable in H2'25. So it's very possible that Copa will achieve 7~8% ASM expansion and 2~3% RASM improvement, translating into double-digit Revenue growth for FY'25.

Copa Airlines Is Undervalued in All Measures

As I mentioned in the beginning, SA Analysts have usually labeled Copa Airlines a Buy or Strong Buy, with Wall St. Analysts doing the same. I find it interesting to see the Price Target that Wall St is giving Copa Airlines and even more interesting the fact that even though the stock has been trading at or below $100 since mid-2023, their Price Target hasn't changed significantly. I think this is a testament to how bullish all analysts are with Copa Airlines, which is translated in an Average Price Target of $147.60, implying a ~50% upside.

When checking the Revenue and EPS Estimate of those analysts, they are targeting a Revenue Growth in line with the ASM growth projected in Copa's 2025 Outlook. This could be a source of improvement throughout the year if my hypothesis that Pricing and FX could add 2~3% in RASM is proven correct. This would also trigger an upward EPS revision, likely pushing the company to a Forward PE below 6x in FY'25.

So if the company does deliver on an 8~10% Revenue Growth and the same Net Income of ~18%, its Net Income will land somewhere between $650M and $700M for a Market Cap of $4.0B. Given its resilient business model, historic high-double digit Net Income and continued delivery of the new Boeing 737 MAX, I believe this company will be rerated to 8x to 10x PE in the near future. This supports the ~50% upside that Wall St Analysts are aiming for, but could even mean an upside of ~75%. This is without mentioning the ~6.5% Dividend Yield that is very well covered by its Free Cash Flow. Those are the reasons why I own Copa Airlines.

Risks to Thesis

I see two fundamental risks to Copa Airlines thesis, both already mentioned in this article. First, the fact that Copa Airlines continues to rely on Boeing's turnaround to receive new aircraft and expand its operations. After significant years of struggle for Boeing, there is some hope on the horizon - however, hope is not something Copa Airlines can use to generate earnings. If those additional 737 MAX doesn't materialize in FY'25, the EPS Estimate will certainly be revised downward. Second, the currency scenario in Latin America is improving, but it could be temporary. If the dollar strengthens again, then Copa Airlines revenue will likely fall, causing the same EPS Estimate downward revision. For now, things are looking good, but it's a risk that investors need to keep a sharp eye on.

Conclusion

In sum, the expected delivery of aircraft in FY'25 and the improvement of FX in Latin America are two tailwinds I believe will support Copa Airlines upside. Even if investors are discounting the exposure Copa Airlines has with Latin America, the fact that it has a resilient business model that generates high-double digit Net Income should be more than enough to merit, at least, an 8x PE. More so when we factor the Dividend Yield of ~6.5%. I believe Copa Airlines is a Buy at current prices and expect the market to take notice during FY'25, probably capturing the 50% upside.

data.title: Itaú Unibanco: Q4, Setting The Stage For A Successful 2025
data.description:
The São Paulo, Brazil-based Itaú Unibanco (NYSE:ITUB) reported its quarterly results on February 2nd, where it once again presented a consistent and solid result, posting record net profits of R$10.9 billion (growing 16% year-over-year) but slightly missing EPS estimates.

Despite a decline in Return on Equity (ROE), the reason was due to equity growth, which, by the way, had a positive effect from capital hedging.

The highlight of the quarter was asset quality, with a drop in defaults, controlled credit costs, and an increase in coverage.

Although the guidance for 2025 came in conservative, without any major surprises given the challenging macroeconomic scenario in Brazil, the expectation is still favorable for the current year, especially with the distribution of an additional R$18 billion between dividends and share buybacks, potentially giving the bank a yield of over 9%.

Last time I covered Itaú Unibanco's ADR, I had adopted a bullish stance based on the bank's leading position versus its incumbent peers.

However, the crux here that has been weighing on Itaú's share performance, which has accumulated a devaluation of about 20% over the course of 2024, has been linked to Brazil's macroeconomic crisis scenario.

In my opinion, the FY24Q4 results reaffirm that Itaú is entering 2025 as the best prepared bank to weather the macro storm, such as dealing with interest rates (Selic) potentially exceeding 15% and still managing, with the lowest levels of defaults, a moderate growth for its credit portfolio and Client margins growth.

Itaú's Q4 Highlights: Well-Positioned for 2025 Challenges

On February 6th, Itaú reported its results for the fourth quarter of 2024, which were once again quite solid and without any major surprises.

Here are the main highlights:

(1) Growth in the credit portfolio, with a 15.5% year-over-year expansion, with small and medium-sized companies being the highlight; (2) Growth in net interest income (NII), with an 8% year-over-year expansion in NII Client, totaling R$28.5 billion, benefiting from the increase in credit volume; (3) Growth in commissions and insurance marking a 6% year-over-year expansion, driven by higher card transaction volumes and a 14% year-over-year increase in asset management and insurance revenues; (4) Cost of credit remained controlled, totaling R$8.6 billion (down 5.5% year-over-year) while delinquency (>90 days) fell 20bps t/t to 2.4%, while the coverage ratio improved 10pp to 215%, reinforcing the strength of the bank's balance sheet; (5) In terms of efficiency, operating expenses expanded by 8.9% year-over-year, but in line with revenues. The efficiency ratio worsened slightly by 0.5pp year-over-year but remains a benchmark in the sector at 38.2%; (6) Return on Equity in Brazil was 23.4% (the best among Brazilian private incumbent banks)—22.2% considering Itaú's operation in Latin America—, jumping 1.6 p.p year-over-year, and CET1 remained solid at 13.7%.

Consolidated 2024 results:

Overall, Itaú ended FY2024 beating its 2023 guidance in all key metrics, with most of them close to its high-end guidance.

Total credit portfolio delivered 15.5% year-over-year growth, up to R$1.35 trillion, well above the initial guidance of 9.5% to 12.5% growth. Financial Margin with Clients at R$108 billion, up 8.3% year-over-year, close to the 2023 high-end guidance of $8.5 billion.

Financial Margin with the Market was R$4.4 billion, within the 2023 guidance of between R$3 billion and R$5 billion.

All in all, it was a very solid fiscal year, very much in line with market expectations, showing that it continues to be the best performing Brazilian incumbent bank.

Guidance for 2025:

Itaú guided a credit portfolio expanding between 4.5% and 8.5%, while net interest income with clients expanding between 7.5% and 11.5%, and net interest income with the market between $1 billion and R$3 billion.

At the center of the guidance, I believe it's fair to assume around $45 billion in profit for 2025 and an ROE of 21.5%, maintaining a trajectory of profitability and consistency.

In my view, the guidance is quite conservative, as it reflects a very challenging macroeconomic scenario, leading the bank to adopt a more cautious stance, with more restrained growth in the loan portfolio and a retraction in Net Interest Income.

The explanation for this softer guidance correlates with the Itaú management team's estimates for 2025, where it expects a deterioration in practically all macroeconomic indicators, as we can see in the table below:

Itaú's Q4 Slightly Misses Analysts' Expectations

Although Itaú's results showed solidity in 2024, possibly the reasons behind a not very optimistic share price reaction can be explained by the fact that it fell slightly below the market's initial estimates.

Itaú reported Q4 EPS of $0.18 cents when the market was expecting $0.19. In fact, this is the fifth consecutive quarter in which Itaú has surprised analysts negatively with regard to its bottom line.

For the full year, Itaú reported EPS of $0.72, also below the initial $0.75 expected by the market.

Although the guidance for 2025 came in soft due to macroeconomic uncertainties, it apparently came in line with market expectations.

Estimates over the last month showed an increase of 1.8% in EPS growth for 2025, now estimated at 9.87% year-over-year, and a further 3.6% in revenues for 2025, estimated at $31.11 billion or ~7% year-over-year growth.

It's worth noting that in the last six months, analysts have revised EPS estimates for 2025 down by about 9% and revenue estimates for the same period down by 3%.

Not surprisingly, ITUB shares have depreciated by around 7.4% over the last six months and 11% over the last twelve months.

Dividends and Shareholder Returns

Itaú also announced in Q4 the payment of R$1.58/share in dividends and Interest on Capital (similar to dividends, but treated differently for tax purposes in Brazil), which corresponds to a dividend yield in 2024 at year-end of an attractive 9%. I say attractive because if we consider an Equity Premium Risk in Brazil of 7.7%, Itaú's yield is still worth it considering the risk of investing in an ADR.

The estimate for FY2025 is to maintain these yields at 9%, with a possible oscillation of 8% in FY2026 and potentially 11% in FY2027. Remember that 2026 is a presidential election year in Brazil and could be a watershed for investor confidence in the country depending on whether a more liberal government, i.e., one with greater fiscal responsibility, can positively reflect in the interest and exchange rate scenario.

Risks

Of course, as a Brazilian bank, the macroeconomic risks of an emerging country are the main ones to consider.

Brazil has struggled to keep inflation within the country's Central Bank target, forcing an interest rate (Selic) above double digits and potentially reaching near high-double teens by 2025, depending on the fiscal direction that the government of current President Lula da Silva has taken.

A prolonged interest rate scenario is challenging for Itaú considering the increase in the cost of credit, reducing demand for loans and financing, and could put pressure on banks' profitability, as well as impacting the growth of the economy and customers' ability to pay.

But also, Brazilian pre-fixed treasury bonds yielding 14.7% with practically minimal risk scare investors away from allocating their capital to variable income assets, such as Brazilian stocks. Therefore, above all, the risk of confidence in Brazilian assets should continue to be the main thing to monitor in periods of high inflation, fiscal uncertainty, and, consequently, high interest rates.

Final Remarks

Overall, Itaú Unibanco starts 2025 very well positioned, especially in the face of the turbulent macro and economic scenario in Brazil, consolidating itself in my view as the most prepared, profitable, and capitalized bank among the incumbents, reinforcing its attractiveness as the best bank to invest in the country.

At the moment, Itaú Unibanco trades at 1.68x its book value, which is a considerable premium to its main peers and reflects exactly the qualities mentioned above. See the table below for a better comparison of this scenario versus the other three main incumbent competitors.

With a dividend yield above the country's equity risk premium, investing in ITUB makes sense both from a shareholder return and value perspective, as it remains well-positioned to thrive in the event of a macroeconomic turnaround for the better, especially with 2026 in sight, which will be an election year in Brazil.

Therefore, I reiterate my bullish stance on Itaú based on compelling dividends, superior quality versus domestic peers, and proven resilience in the face of challenging economic scenarios.

data.title: Constellation Brands: Berkshire Hathaway Defies Tariff Concerns With A Stake
data.description:
Constellation Brands, Inc. (NYSE:STZ) (NEOE:STZ:CA) produces and sells a portfolio of alcoholic drinks, including the Modelo and Corona beer brands, spirits like Casa Noble Tequila and Mi Campo, and a number of wine brands. The stock has recently attracted Warren Buffett’s interest, as Buffett’s investment giant Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (BRK.A) bought a $1.24 billion stake in the company during Q4.

The bought position follows a downward trend for Constellation’s stock. Constellation’s financial performance has mostly remained stable amid weakness in the industry, but the most recent quarterly report did show increasing signs of weakening demand in the spirit & wine category. Combined with major concerns over a potential 25% tariff on Mexican imports, the stock has lost -34% of its value after I upgraded my rating to Hold in my previous July 2024 article on the stock, titled “Constellation Brands: Now Fairly Valued As Q1 Continues Growth Momentum”. The return compares to S&P 500’s return of 10% in the same period.

Warren Buffett’s Berkshire Hathaway Buys a Stake in Constellation Brands

With institutional investment managers filing 13-F filings to disclose fund holdings at the end of Q4, Warren Buffett’s investment giant Berkshire Hathaway revealed a notable stake in Constellation among the company’s other trades. The company has acquired around 5.6 million shares in Constellation during Q4, making the position worth around $1.24 billion at the end of Q4. Of Berkshire Hathaway’s holdings in publicly traded stocks, the new stake in Constellation is the 19th largest, worth 0.5% of the company’s combined portfolio. The stake represents around 3% of Constellation’s outstanding shares.

Interestingly, Constellation isn’t Berkshire Hathaway’s only position in an alcohol producer. Berkshire Hathaway previously bought a stake in major alcohol conglomerate Diageo (DEO) in Q1 of 2023, which the company has since held on to. Notably, though, Berkshire Hathaway seems to hold much better conviction in Constellation – the stake in Diageo is only worth $29 million.

Tariff Concerns, Industry Weakness Weigh on Historically Stable Earnings Growth

After very stable earnings growth in the long term, Constellation’s financial performance has had very slight shadows against a weak industry backdrop. After still stronger previous quarters, the most recent fiscal Q3 report turned more sour – revenues declined -0.4% to $2.46 billion after historically moderate but stable growth, and operating income declined -2.0% to $802 million with both sales and earnings missing Wall Street’s expectations and causing the stock’s most wide single-day decline in a decade.

Notably, the company’s beer portfolio has still performed very well as Pacifico sales continue to grow, and the larger beer brands continue to defend and even expand already significant market positions. In Q3, beer category sales grew 3% to $2.03 billion and earnings stood strong at a 37.9% operating margin, down a slight 0.6 percentage points year-on-year due to increased marketing spend. Total industry beer sales are still expected to grow going forward in the US, albeit at a slow pace, and Constellation has performed very well in the category. According to Nielsen’s data, Constellation’s good beer category performance has continued into the fiscal Q4 period as well.

Causing the financial slowdown and a weaker outlook, Constellation’s spirit & wine portfolio has performed very poorly against an already weak industry. The category’s sales declined -14% in Q3 to $431 million, and the operating margin declined 3.3 percentage points to 22.1%. The category’s results have already been weak previously, but the Q3 performance deepened along with industry-wide issues; younger people are drinking less and consumers have generally been cautious, for example leading to Brown-Forman’s (BF.B) declining sales and recent layoffs, and Diageo’s recently declining US sales among other spirit producers’ struggles.

While the overall earnings performance has so far still been quite stable, as a more notable threat, the potential tariff on Mexico could have a major impact on Constellation. Around 85% of the company’s revenues are generated from Mexican imports, and the company generates almost all of its revenues from the US – a 25% tariff by President Donald Trump on Mexican goods would cause major pressure on Constellation’s earnings, as the tariff would impose major cost pressure on Constellation with an additional 25% payment on input costs from Mexican imports to the US. Although Constellation could push some of the additional cost onto consumers, the company’s brands would likely lose ground against US-produced beers and spirits. The tariff on Mexico is still currently on hold as implementation is being postponed amid political discussions; the tariff may ultimately never come into effect, which would be a major positive for Constellation as the tariff risk is significant for the company.

Ultimately, despite the looming risks in industry-wide sales and tariffs, Constellation should continue to generate very healthy earnings. The company’s Q3 operating margin still stood high at 32.5%, making overall minor sales turbulence not as large of a threat. Constellation’s great beer portfolio has also continued to show strong momentum against industry trends, and the severely weak spirit and wine portfolio only represents less than a fifth of Constellation’s overall sales. The tariff risk seems more notable than the spirit industry’s weakness, but its implementation and impact on Constellation largely remain uncertain.

Berkshire Hathaway’s currently disclosed position in Constellation was built during Q4, prior to the most recent quarterly report and recent tariff discussions – the company likely valued Constellation’s longstanding, stable earnings growth at a historically quite low valuation, as Constellation’s forward P/E now stands moderate at 12.1. The stake did still defy Trump’s tariff proposals that were already previously announced in late 2024.

Constellation’s Valuation Remains Balanced Due To The Uncertainties

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Constellation’s stock, now estimating a slightly worse long-term industry performance and pricing in potential tariff impacts.

Due to uncertainties in the spirit industry and from tariffs, I estimate Constellation’s revenue growth to have a slight hiccup in the next couple of years with growth of just 1.5% in FY2026, and slightly slower 3.6% growth in FY2027. Afterwards, I still expect Constellation’s strong beer portfolio to drive growth, contributing to a 3.3% CAGR from FY2024 to FY2034. After FY2034, I lowered my perpetual growth estimate to 2% due to industry concerns.

The tariffs look to add margin pressure on Constellation as the full price impact may not be passed onto consumers. I estimate the EBIT margin to fall to 31.5% from an estimated 34.0% in FY2025, still staying high despite spirit margin concerns also contributing towards margin pressure.

Constellation’s free cash flow should be relatively stable, but likely to be somewhat pressured by heightened capital expenditures and working capital increases.

The estimates put Constellation’s fair value estimate at $177.6, 9% above the stock price at the time of writing. Although the stock has fallen very notably after my previous article, I again believe that the stock is roughly fairly valued with a fair margin of safety. The weaker industry outlook and potential tariff on Mexico are priced in as considerable risks for a reason, while I believe that Constellation’s earnings should overall stay quite stable regardless. The potential tariff’s impact is still very uncertain, and could also cause a much smaller or higher earnings hiccup.

The fair value estimate is down from $235.66 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 7.24%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate a 3.61% interest rate, annualized from the most recent quarter’s interest expenses and debt. As Constellation’s equity valuation has declined, I now estimate a slightly higher 30% long-term debt-to-equity, still being moderate.

For cost of equity, I use the 10-year bond yield of 4.48% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.33% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for the US. I have kept the beta estimate at 0.56. With an ESG add-on of 1.5% and a liquidity premium of 0.2%, the cost of equity stands at 8.60% and the WACC at 7.24%.

Takeaway

Berkshire Hathaway bought a notable stake in Constellation during Q4, likely valuing the company’s long history of stably growing earnings. At the same time, though, the current earnings outlook has two weak points after recent developments – especially the spirit industry has shown weakness in the US, carrying onto Constellation’s already weak performance in the relatively small segment. At the same time, Constellation manufactures the clear majority of its products in Mexico, and Trump’s potential tariffs could add considerable pressure on either sales volume or margins, depending on Constellation’s pricing strategy.

The stock has fallen considerably and already prices in the noted risks to a good degree. As such, I remain at a Hold rating for Constellation Brands.

data.title: FUNL: A Nice Upgrade Over Russell 1000 Value ETFs, So Why Sell?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The CornerCap Fundametrics Large-Cap ETF (BATS:FUNL) might not have much name recognition, but it's a nice upgrade over many larger funds, including its benchmark, represented by the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD). I'll demonstrate this by analyzing its strategy and fundamentals in depth, but I caution current and prospective investors that FUNL plans to liquidate in less than one year. It might be best to sell or avoid and direct your assets to one of the other four peers I'll highlight today. I hope you enjoy the read.

FUNL Overview

Strategy Discussion

CornerCap is a boutique asset management firm with a proprietary strategy for evaluating securities called "Fundametrics." Although proprietary, FUNL's statutory prospectus provides some good information on the firm's approach, which I've summarized below.

1. The initial selection universe is companies with market caps within the range of the Russell 1000 Index "at the time of purchase."

2. The "Fundametrics" system evaluates approximately 500 stocks against their custom peer groups on 120 fundamental factors, including value, growth, momentum, quality, and risk, to create an "Alpha Composite" score. A stock's value features are most important, as well as earnings growth rates and cash flow measurements. The top 30% proceed to the next step.

3. Remaining stocks must pass a "Financial Warnings Overlay" test designed to identify high-risk stocks. CornerCap uses a separate screening system that reviews metrics like earnings quality, capital structure, profitability, and sentiment indicators.

4. The advisor is permitted to buy stocks that pass both the "Alpha Composite" and "Financial Warnings Overlay" tests while considering diversification.

Pending Liquidation

The process sounds extremely comprehensive, and without spoiling things too much, I was able to confirm that FUNL's fundamental statistics were strong relative to its benchmark. However, the prospectus also notes the following:

Pursuant to the terms of an Asset Purchase Agreement dated July 28, 2024, the Adviser sold its advisory account assets to EP Wealth Advisors for cash consideration in a transaction that closed on September 13, 2024...the Adviser will continue to manage the Fund for a finite period of time, which is anticipated to conclude with the Fund's liquidation and termination at the end of January 2026.

Liquidating won't cost you your whole investment. However, if you opt to hold on until the last minute, you'll likely receive a sub-optimal payout. Fidelity notes you'll likely experience capital gains taxes and uneven tracking (i.e., market price is below the basket's net asset value) as you get closer to the liquidation date. Fidelity also notes how "one company even had the gall to stick shareholders with the legal costs of closing the fund." While rare, CornerCap leaves open that possibility, broadly stating:

Shareholders remaining in the Fund during the Final Liquidation Period may bear increased transaction expenses incurred in connection with the final disposition of the Fund's portfolio holdings.

My simple advice is to save yourself the trouble and get out now. There's no advantage to waiting, especially when there are other viable options. Let's discuss those next.

FUNL Analysis

Peer Group and Performance

The four ETFs listed below are three potential alternatives to FUNL, though IWD is the least attractive, as I'll demonstrate shortly.

  1. iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD)
  2. Vanguard Value ETF (VTV)
  3. Avantis US Large Cap Value ETF (AVLV)
  4. Fidelity Enhanced Large Cap Value ETF (FELV)

FUNL's 0.50% expense ratio is high, but that's pretty normal for an actively managed fund, and I suppose it doesn't matter from a total returns perspective if the strategy is good enough to offset the difference.

As the chart below shows, it's certainly done that compared to its benchmark, IWD, outperforming by 10.52% (87.61% vs. 77.09%) since its inception on August 19, 2020.

However, the lower-cost options (VTV and FELV) did just as well, so there are clearly several ways investors can outperform what I consider to be one of the weaker large-cap value benchmarks. Consider that over the last ten years through January 2025, IWD's 142% total return ranked #33/43 in its category, with many dividend-focused and broad-based value funds performing better (e.g., DGRW, DGRO, FNDB, SPYV).

FUNL Fundamental Analysis

To see how, it's helpful to examine the fundamentals of each fund. They tell a pretty good story of what each ETF does well and, more importantly, why IWD needs improvement. Here are some selected fundamental metrics for FUNL's top 25 sub-industries, which comprise 60.65% of the portfolio. Have a quick scan, and I'll provide my takeaways afterward.

FUNL doesn't really do much wrong. Its 1.03 five-year beta is close to IWD's 1.00, there are meaningful improvements in sales and earnings growth, its valuation ratios are low, and its quality statistics are solid. Let's explore some of these qualities in greater detail.

1. The "Fundametrics" approach likely considers historical and forward-looking metrics, and from a growth perspective, the team has successfully "enhanced" IWD. Its 8.78% five-year historical growth rate is 2.18% higher, and its one-year estimated earnings per share growth rate of 11.04% is 2.75% greater and the best in this sample. Lastly, FUNL's 5.10/10 sector-adjusted growth score, which I derived from the Seeking Alpha Factor Grades of its individual components, is also the best. FELV closely follows with a 4.88/10 score.

2. FUNL trades at 15.34x forward earnings and 10.84x trailing cash flow. These metrics are also impressive, especially when considering FUNL overweights Technology stocks and underweights Financials, as summarized in the sector allocation table below.

Technology stocks are usually more expensive, but FUNL's top holding in this sector is Cisco Systems (CSCO), which trades at just 17.19x forward earnings and 18.69x trailing cash flow. Approximately 50% of its tech sector holdings (6.62% by weight) have forward P/Es below 20x compared to 30% for IWD, so there's clearly an effort to avoid pricier stocks like Accenture plc (ACN), IBM (IBM), and Texas Instruments (TXN).

3. FUNL's quality metrics are strongest overall. As the table shows, its sector-adjusted profit score of 8.92/10 is the highest, and that's most supported by its 16.10% free cash flow margins. Recall how the "Fundametrics" strategy emphasizes cash flow metrics, so this finding isn't too surprising. To be sure, its return on assets and equity metrics are lower than AVLV's, but in the case of the latter, you'll have to accept higher volatility based on its 1.16 five-year beta. If you were to switch investments, this might not suit your style.

4. I believe FELV is the best of the four alternatives, but VTV is a solid all-around fund worth considering. The reason is that, like FUNL, it doesn't do a lot wrong. To illustrate, consider how VTV ranks in my ETF ranking system on ten key factors:

  • Expenses: #3/120
  • Liquidity: #12/120
  • Diversification: #16/120
  • Risk: #48/120
  • Size: #81/120
  • Growth: #42/120
  • Value: #79/120
  • Quality: #50/120
  • Momentum: #38/120
  • Sentiment: #67/120

As shown, VTV doesn't rank in the bottom quartile on any key factor except for size, which is just a function of its higher $252 billion weighted average market cap. Therefore, you can expect consistent, steady, and slightly above-average returns, and that's exactly what VTV has delivered over the long run. Recall how IWD's ten-year 142% total return ranks #33/43 in the large-cap value category. Well, VTV's 182% total return ranks #10/43, and it's because it's designed to handle a variety of market environments. If you're a current FUNL shareholder and not sure where to go, VTV is a pretty safe option.

Investment Recommendation

It's a shame FUNL plans to liquidate, and despite its strong fundamentals, I've assigned it a "sell" rating to encourage readers to explore alternatives. I think VTV is a reasonably safe choice, though AVLV and FELV are more likely to deliver enhanced long-term returns despite their higher expense ratios.

data.title: PFLD: With Spreads Tight, This Preferred Equity ETF Is No Longer Attractive (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

We wrote about the AAM Low Duration Preferred and Income Securities ETF (NYSEARCA:PFLD) more than a year ago, when we argued the fund was attractive given prevailing market yields and low portfolio duration. The name has delivered since our last rating:

The ETF is up over +10% on a total return basis since our 'Buy' rating, mainly driven by its dividend yield, as we can see from the table above. While the price point is virtually the same, the total return is high, indicating the dividend income.

We are going to revisit the exchange-traded fund given the stretched overall market conditions, and highlight why we are downgrading the name to 'Hold', no longer believing the fund presents an attractive entry point.

The fund composition thematic lies with a low duration

What sets this ETF apart from other preferred securities funds is its overall low portfolio duration, which clocks in at 1.28 years. A low duration translates into a low sensitivity to interest rate changes and credit spread changes. A low duration also makes this fund more of a dividend vehicle:

There are 165 names in the ETF's portfolio, and they come from companies with a mean market capitalization of $344 mm. The fund is overweight banks and financial institutions:

  • Banking 28.5%
  • Financial Services 20.7%
  • Real Estate 20.2%
  • Insurance 12.6%
  • Consumer Goods 5.3%

Given the sectoral composition, it should not be a surprise what we see in the top names:

Goldman and JPMorgan are the top holdings in this fund, with further top names falling in the financial services category.

Sensitivities and historic returns

While the fund can target what it believes to be lower duration securities, kindly keep in mind that preferred equity in the financials space is usually perpetual. Market participants generally follow the first call date convention to get an implied duration, but it does not always function like that:

When benchmarked against the iShares Preferred and Income Securities ETF (PFF), we can observe a very close correlation. PFLD had a deep -14% drawdown in 2022, despite its stated low duration. The only time it significantly outperformed PFF was during the challenging times of 2023, when its drawdown since inception only moved to -16% versus roughly -20% for PFF. The above chart translates into the realities of the preferred market - since there are no stated maturity dates, securities tend to see their duration increase during times of stress.

Let us re-do the same graph using a total return feature that also incorporates dividend income:

The two names have very comparable returns in the past three years, with PFF actually coming on top. More importantly, the lines are very well correlated, indicating that the implied lower duration for PFLD is not really consistent with reality. Kindly remember the definition of duration - for a 2-year duration, PFLD should only experience -2% drawdown when spreads or risk-free rates move up by 100 bps. The fund, however, has been much more volatile than that.

Preferred market is tight

As a rule of thumb for any fixed income instrument, an investor should buy it when spreads are very wide and there is some sort of dislocation. For example, the March 2023 banking crisis would have provided for an ideal entry point into this fund. Back then, preferred shares for financial institutions were pricing in that vertical default probability we saw for some of the financial institutions that went under. Not in today's market. Let us look at a series of preferred shares issued by Morgan Stanley in the summer of 2024:

MS.PR.Q is up since issuance despite rates being higher. This denotes a macro environment where spreads have contracted tremendously, compensating for the move higher in rates. When it was issued in July 2024, 5-year risk-free rates were at 4%, so the preferred was priced at T+2.625% (the series has a coupon of 6.625%). This pricing method is based on the first call date, assuming it will be called at that point in time. Rates in that node are up to 4.35% now, yet the preferred shares trade at a premium. We therefore have an implied spread of sub 200 bps for Morgan Stanley. Very well rated Tier 1 banks trade at sub 200 bp spreads now, which is a significant contraction versus the past years, and even the summer of 2024.

We have pounded the table again and again on the tightness of spreads across the high yield and investment grade sectors, but it is the case now for preferred equities as well. Given the lack of compensation for the risk taken, a patient investor should wait and hold until a better entry point develops. While nobody can time the market, an investor can develop a sense for attractive macro conditions which warrant an entry or exit from an investment.

Conclusion

PFLD is a fixed income exchange-traded fund. The name tries to differentiate itself via a short duration profile, but that feature is debatable in the preferred equity market. While issuers do tend to call securities on the first call date, it is not mandatory. Furthermore, interest rate stress or credit stress in the market cause preferred equities to have a higher duration because they end up not being called right away. PFLD has performed in the past years very much in line with PFF; thus, its market implied duration is longer than stated. With spreads very tight for Tier 1 financial institutions, today's price point is not attractive for an entry anymore. We are thus downgrading PFLD to 'Hold'.

data.title: EWG: German Stocks' Massive Rally Likely To Pause (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Despite all the tariff talk, European stocks are suddenly all the rage. The major overseas bourses have outperformed the S&P 500 to begin 2025, and no spot has garnered more macro attention than what’s going on in Germany. The Xetra DAX closed last week at an all-time high as it appears the European Central Bank will be aggressively cutting rates in the next 12 months while the US Federal Reserve may be on hold.

The DAX has now gained more than 32% from a year ago, which is better than the S&P 500’s 30% total return. It’s important to point out that the major German stock index is a total return index whereas the SPX is price only. Also, there are currency swings at play. The euro has weakened against the US dollar over the past 12 months, but it seems to be holding the $1.02-$1.03 zone.

Today, I’m revisiting the iShares MSCI Germany ETF (NYSEARCA:EWG). I was bullish on the product way back in November of 2022. Since then, the fund has returned 62%, actually beating the S&P 500 by a few percentage points. With EWG now more expensive compared to Q4 of 2022, I’m downgrading EWG to a hold. The chart is also more suspect with major long-term resistance in play.

German DAX Soaring to All-Time Highs, +32.3% YoY

EWG Among the YTD Country ETF Winners

According to the issuer, EWG seeks to track the investment results of an index composed of German equities. It offers investors exposure to large and mid-sized companies in Germany and is used to express a single-country view.

Now ranked in the top 4% in its Asset Class according to Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, EWG has significant price strength. Surprising to me, the ETF’s assets under management is just $1.0 billion while the annual expense ratio is moderate at 50 basis points, as of February 14, 2025. There’s no stated dividend yield, but historically the fund has paid out $0.70-$0.80 annually, so I contend that the true yield is likely around 2% or just a bit more.

Where EWG shines today is with its share-price momentum. Now A+ rated on the ETF Grading system, it has been a remarkable turnaround from just a handful of months ago when the fund was merely trading sideways. It’s a risky portfolio, however, considering historical volatility trends and its concentrated holding mix. Finally, EWG is highly liquid given almost 900,000 shares traded daily and its 30-day median bid/ask spread of three basis points, per iShares.

For a portfolio refresher, the allocation plots along the top row of the style box, indicating its high weight to large caps. Unlike much of Europe, the German stock market is home to many more growth stocks than value, so the DAX may have a higher correlation to the S&P 500 and Dow Jones Industrial Average than the other major European markets.

Where I have a bit of a concern now is with the price-to-earnings ratio which is near 16x. That’s certainly not high on its own, but just 27 months ago it was more than three turns less expensive. Also consider that the ETF’s long-term earnings growth rate is just 7.9%, so the resulting PEG ratio is not far from two. These figures don’t scream “expensive,” but they are also no longer in bargain territory.

EWG: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

In terms of positioning, like the US stock market, Information Technology is the largest sector, but it’s only 22%; the S&P 500 has a more than 30% weight to I.T. You’ll also find heavy exposure to the cyclical-value areas of Industrials and Financials.

Financials, in particular, have performed very well in the past 52 weeks, which has been a boon for EWG. It has also benefitted from having no Energy exposure – the oil & gas space has been hampered by low WTI and Brent oil prices.

EWG: Holdings & Dividend Information

Seasonally, the second half of February has been a tough stretch for EWG over the past 10 years, so we could see the product pare some of its year-to-date gains soon.

Beyond that, the March through May period has been bullish when analyzing the past 10 years of performance data.

EWG: Bullish March-May Seasonality

The Technical Take

Leading me to downgrade EWG from a buy to a hold is its chart. Notice in the graph below that the fund is right back at obvious resistance in the $35 to $37 range. Until proven otherwise, the current price north of $36 is a selling opportunity.

Longer-term, if we see a breakout, then massive upside potential is there. A measured move bullish target to the mid-high $50s would absolutely be in play based on the large $20 trading range since way back in the mid-2000s. This is certainly something to watch as the year progresses.

Also take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average – it's rising in its slope, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. Of course, we’ve seen this before (2007, 2018, 2021). Moreover, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart is in technical overbought conditions, but it has been ranging in a bullish zone between 40 and 80 for the last several months.

Finally, with a high amount of volume by price under today’s price, there should be ample support if we see a protracted pullback.

EWG: Shares Right at Major Long-Term Resistance

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on EWG. The valuation is fair and cheap compared to the S&P 500, but the earnings growth trend is not impressive. With the ETF at technical resistance, booking gains after a very strong two-plus years is prudent.

data.title: Life, Liberty, And The Pursuit Of Returns: Celestica, My Top President's Day Stock
data.description:
Is the Stock Market Open on President’s Day?

This Monday, the New York Stock Exchange, Nasdaq, and bond markets will be closed for trading in observance of President's Day. But a day off from trading can be a great time to assess how your portfolio is doing so far in 2025.

This President’s Day, the market is giving us plenty of reasons to feel patriotic, and Top Stocks worth exploring ranked by Seeking Alpha’s Quant System. One of my top President’s Day picks and an Alpha Picks stock: Celestica Inc. (NYSE:CLS), the AI-driven electronic manufacturing services company is making waves in the data center space.

Celestica’s Star-Spangled Rally

Celestica Inc. takes its name from the stars, and its rise over the past year and a half has been nothing short of celestial. Formerly a subsidiary of IBM, CLS has since evolved to become a market leader in electronic manufacturing services and a key technological partner for large-scale data centers, otherwise known as hyperscalers and other AI infrastructure providers. A two-time Alpha Pick, CLS continues to be one of our top-performing stocks, consecutively topping earnings expectations in Q4 2024, with revenues of $2.55B and adjusted EPS of $1.11.

First selected as an Alpha Pick on October 16, 2023, at $25.65 per share, CLS is currently trading at $131.02 per share, and has returned over 384% since its addition to the AP portfolio and is up +236% over the last year.

Celestica Inc. 1-year Trading Chart

CLS has 18 manufacturing facilities in North America and Asia where it produces a variety of products under its Advanced Technology Solutions (ATS) and Connectivity & Cloud Solutions (CCS) segments. CLS specializes in AI infrastructure manufacturing and design, which includes data center technologies like networking switches, servers, and storage. Celestica’s CCS segment has ballooned to account for 67% of total revenues and has grown 42% YoY as supercharged demand for artificial intelligence has prompted tech companies to spend big on massive data centers that can handle the sheer level of computing power necessary to keep up in the AI arms race.

CLS Quant Rating History (as of 2/14/25)

Seeking Alpha Quant Ratings offers tools to simplify investment processes. Quant Ratings aim to provide an objective, unemotional, data-driven evaluation of each stock based on over 100 underlying metrics graded across five factors: Valuation, Growth, Profitability, Momentum, and EPS Revisions. Celestica is Quant Rated Strong Buy, so let’s dive into why.

Celestica Inc. (NYSE: CLS)

  • Market Capitalization: $15.1B

  • Quant Rating: Strong Buy

  • Quant Sector Ranking (as of 2/14/25): 7 out of 549

  • Quant Industry Ranking (as of 2/14/25): 1 out of 16

CLS currently is ranked the #7 Technology stock and the #1 Electronic Manufacturing Services stock. Celesticahas continued to top the charts, even in what has been a volatile year thus far for U.S. tech companies. Like many of its technology peers, the Toronto, Canada-based company was dealt a blow by the sudden debut of low-cost Chinese AI model DeepSeek last month, losing more than 32.8% between January 24-27 as the chatbot’s launch wiped nearly $1T in value from the U.S. stock market. But, CLS has already charted a recovery, gaining 16.5% more than its pre-DeepSeek valuation by February 5. Following the initial DeepSeek dip, CLS has been projected by some Wall Street analysts as a potential winner from the whole debacle, bolstered by the inevitable rise in AI spending—and thus, increase in demand for more AI infrastructure—that the unexpected competition from China has already triggered across the global technology sector. The Canadian tech innovator upped its guidance for 2025 at the end of January, now expecting revenue to be $10.7B compared to its previous outlook of $10.4B, as tech companies’ insatiable appetite for AI data center space fuels demand for its specialized brand of connectivity solutions.

SA Factor Grades rate investment characteristics on a sector-relative basis. CLS has scored A+’s in Momentum and EPS revisions, a B+ in Growth, a B- in Valuation, and a C+ in Profitability.

CLS Growth

CLS’s growth metrics speak for themselves: forward EPS diluted growth of 34.2%, a 245.9% difference from the 10% sector median. The company’s forward EPS GAAP growth is 35.6%, a 215.6% from the 11.3% sector median, and forward revenue growth of 16.8% is a 162.9% difference to illustrate consistent growth and forward-looking potential across the board.

CLS Stock’s Valuation

CLS has maintained an attractive valuation despite its riveting rally, with a forward PEG of 0.77—a -58.6% difference from the broader sector—showcasing a compelling discount to its peers in a period of otherwise frothy tech valuations. Its one year price performance of 238.2% is a stunning 3,696.6% difference from the 6.3% sector median. CLS’ valuation underpins its ‘A+’ Momentum Grade while its 'B+' Growth Grade is pushed higher by forward EPS growth of 49% and YoY EPS diluted growth of 88%, a more than 1000% difference from the sector median.

Celestica: Why I’m Betting on the Data Center Boom This President’s Day

CLS posted strong financial results in Q3 ‘24, reporting a 19% YoY increase in revenue to $2.55B, compared to $2.14B in Q4 202 and a 58% YoY increase in adjusted EPS. Celestica is also projecting $10.7B in 2025 revenue and 22% EPS growth. Read more from SA’s latest Earnings Call Insights.

CLS & Peers 1Y Performance vs. S&P 500 (as of 2/14/25)

CLS continues to outpace its biggest rivals, including Flex Ltd (FLEX), Jabil Inc. (JBL) Sanmina Corporation (SANM), and Plexus Corp (PLXS), by more than 4x each as a result of its powerful momentum, with one-year price performance of 246%, a 1,987.3% difference from the 11.8% sector median, but there are still risks to consider.

Risks to Consider

Despite CLS’s stellar momentum, robust growth, and impressive Q4 results, several insider transactions suggest potential headwinds. Celestica is a Canadian-based company, and as tariffs continue to be headline news, particularly 25% tariffs on imports from Canada, which likely had some impact on February 6th, when CLS stock fell by around 7%. The stock’s slide may have also been driven by reports that the company’s CEO Robert Mionis had sold 75,000 shares of CLS stock. Certain Wall Street analysts, including SA’s Danil Cereda, downgraded their assessment on CLS to a Hold out of caution.

Also worth noting is CLS’s C+ Profitability Score, which has been weighed down by factors including a gross profit margin of 10.7%, a -78.86% difference from the 50.1% sector median. CLS also has a trailing levered free cash flow margin (FCF) of 3.25%, a -72.4% difference to the 11.8% sector median. However, an A+- asset turnover ratio of 1.62x shows that the company continues to reinvest heavily in its future operations.

Concluding Summary

If you have time to sit down and explore some potential new investments this long weekend, CLS makes for a compelling growth/value play thanks to its supercharged momentum, fantastic earnings results, and the SA Quant Team’s Strong Buy rating. This President’s Day, enjoy life, liberty, and the pursuit of returns.

Alternatively, if you're seeking a limited number of monthly ideas from the hundreds of top quant Strong Buy-rated stocks, consider exploring Alpha Picks. Alpha Picks employs a transparent and systematic strategy that combines the expertise of SA’s Quantitative Team, our unique data, and the computational power of the systems we have built. Let SA’s Quant Team do the research for you in building a market-beating, data-driven portfolio for long-term growth.

data.title: Sun Life Financial: A Drama-Free Growth Story
data.description:
Sun Life Financial: A Buy Despite A Disastrous Q4

When I first started my analysis on Sun Life Financial Inc. (TSE:SLF, NYSE:SLF), its Q4'24 report was due in a few days. Admittedly, I was optimistic about the stock, but the latest report came, and I was tempted to downgrade it to a hold. However, given the reasons for the challenging quarter, I’m convinced that we have an even better buying opportunity instead.

Company Overview

Sun Life Financial Inc. is a financial services company that offers its services in many countries. It built remarkable momentum in Asian Markets like Hong Kong and the Philippines while maintaining a solid market presence in Europe. However, most of its business comes from North America.

Sun Life Financial offers three primary services: Wealth and Asset Management, Group Health and Protection, and Individual Protection Services.

Sun Life Segment Weight By Revenue (FY2024)

Note: All dollar amounts are presented as CAD, and in $millions unless otherwise stated.

Business Segment

Revenue

Percentage

Wealth Sales & Asset Management

196,074

97.17%

Group - Health & Protection

2,737

1.36%

Individual Protection

2,983

1.48%

Total

201,794

100%

Source: Sun Life Financial

Recent Financial Performance

Sun Life Financial's Q4'24 financials present mixed signals, which is the primary reason for my initial “hold” rating. Its reported quarterly net income significantly decreased by 68.36% to $237 million—one of the most concerning declines in the report and reported ROE decreased from 14% to 4% year-over-year.

The full-year net income grew 3.43% to $3.86 billion, thanks mostly to its Q2 and Q3 results. However, the dismal Q4 caused the company to miss its quarterly and full-year estimates.

Sun Life’s Full-Year Revenue Per Region/Segment

(24.91%)

Source: Sun Life 2024 Annual Report

Sun Life faces both headwinds and pockets of opportunity across its segments. Although most geographical segments show declines, Asset Management's 8.66% growth presents as a silver lining, though it alone can’t yet offset the rest. The more notable declines in Asia (-12.43%) and Corporate (-24.91%) segments. However, what caught my attention is the firm’s home market in Canada – it decreased by 9.17%, which potentially represents near-term challenges that need observation.

U.S. Market

On the other hand, its U.S. business operations experienced a minimal 2.96% decline. Though it’s not a growth driver, the 2.96% decrease isn’t the end of the world, considering it's in among the costliest places for healthcare.

In fact, the reason for the decline in the U.S. was due to “unfavorable morbidity experience in medical stop-loss,” indicating a high volume of claims in the country. Call me an optimist, but unless there’s an ongoing apocalypse, these things don’t drag on for quarters upon quarters, so I think I can safely say this is a temporary setback. Further, when an insurer loses money on claims, they will increase premiums - just as what would happen to your car insurance if you had enough “expensive” and at-fault claims.

Sun Life Financial Inc. Against its Peers

While it’s true that Sun Life is currently experiencing some tough times compared to its previous quarters, let’s see how it measures up against its closest competitors.

5-YR Net Income

Source: Seeking Alpha

As you can see from the chart above, Sun Life Financial’s net income is on a relatively stable trajectory with a slight positive trend over the past 5 years. The company’s net income is also more consistent than its peers, like PFG, which shows a concerning decline to negative territory. Or, MFC, which experienced notable volatility with a pretty sharp dip in 2022.

Sun Life’s notable competitor is Prudential, with a higher net income of over $4 billion. However, Prudential's earnings appear much more volatile than many investors might prefer.

5-YR Dividend History

Sun Life Financial's forward annual dividend of $2.35 reflects a healthy 4.31% yield. Its payout ratio of 47.04% is also on the low side, which means the firm provides sufficient shareholder value while leaving enough room for reinvestment in the business—or, more realistically, as a buffer against headwinds.

The ratio may be relatively low at first glance, but it’s a somewhat different perspective when you consider Sun Life’s peers.

Companies/Peers

Div. Yield (FWD)

Annual Payout (FWD)

Payout Ratio

Sun Life Financial (SLF)

4.31%

$2.35

47.04%

Prudential Financial (PRU)

4.83%

$5.40

40.15%

Principal Financial Group (PFG)

3.45%

$2.92

40.61%

Manulife Financial (MFC)

3.78%

$1.12

42.19%

Unum Group (UNM)

2.23%

$1.68

19.25%

Though Sun Life’s payout ratio is higher on this selection, 47% is still well below the threshold of concern, and it is the second-highest when it comes to yields. Investors will also appreciate that the company has maintained an attractive 5-year CAGR dividend growth rate of 8.45%.

Is SLF Stock a Buy?

Source: Seeking Alpha

SLF stock trades at around 10.59x forward earnings, and the stock is up about 17% over the past five years. It’s up over 10% in the last year alone (including the recent dip), not including dividends.

Here’s how Sun Life compares P/E-wise to its competitors:

Companies/Peers

P/E GAAP (FWD)

Sun Life Financial (SLF)

10.59

Prudential Financial (PRU)

7.87

Principal Financial Group (PFG)

10.37

Manulife Financial (MFC)

11.24

Unum Group (UNM)

8.23

Peer P/E Ratio Median

10.37

Source: Seeking Alpha

When I first started my analysis on Sun Life, the stock was up 20% in the last five years. It lost roughly 8% after the Q4 announcement. That said, the stock is a bit higher than its peer group, suggesting it’s fairly valued.

Next Potential Catalyst: Asian Markets

Although circumstances were a bit unfavorable, Sun Life’s business in Asia appears well-positioned to be its next potential growth driver for the firm. Its operations in Asia went from over $3 billion in net loss to a $3.35 billion gain from December 2022 to December 2024, which reflected around 217% over the said period. In my book, this is nothing short of remarkable.

However, Sun Life Asia’s net income declined 20.61% from $592 million to $470 million year-over-year, which includes a one-time impairment charge of $186 million in its Vietnam business. Regardless, net income is still up by 123.8% from its 2022 levels of $210 million, and in my view, what’s more important is how they’re positioning themselves for future growth.

The firm doesn’t seem to take the typical Western approach of treating Asia as an afterthought. Sun Life wants to accelerate its momentum in Asia. The firm has established its foothold in eight major Asian markets and serves 30 million clients. This is more than a “growth opportunity” in markets where the middle class is growing like wildfire, and financial services are highly sought-after.

Source: Sun Life Financial

Indeed, the business model is well-accepted for these markets. Sun Life has struck a balance between wealth management (42%), protection products (30%), and group benefits (28%). And, in my experience, this level of diversification is perfect for most emerging economies – you have wealth management for the growing affluent class, basic protection products for first-time buyers, and group benefits for the expanding corporate sector.

But of course, Sun Life has an established presence in 28 markets worldwide, and with $1.54 trillion in assets under management, it has the means to execute its objectives in Asia.

Insurance Sales In Asian Countries (Q4’24)

Let’s have a more granular look at the sales figures across its operations in Asia. The Hong Kong Market takes the lead with over $1.42 billion in insurance sales as of Q4’24, which represents a 92.57% increase from the recorded sales of Q3’24. I think it aligns with the company's diversified strategy, as Hong Kong's financial market demands all three of its services.

The firm's positioning in high-growth potential markets is also noteworthy – India and the Philippines pulling in sales of $319 million and $242 million, respectively.

Asian Countries

Insurance Sales

Hong Kong

$1.425 Billion

India

$319 Million

Philippines

$242 Million

Malaysia

$74 Million

Vietnam

$66 Million

Indonesia

$58 Million

China

$36 Million

Other Asian Countries

$223 Million

Source: Sun Life Financial

And, although moderate as compared to others, Malaysia, China, and Indonesia have substantial room for expansion – all these markets have a rapidly growing economy and increasing financial literacy, which means untapped potential that could drive Sun Life's next phase of growth, and also the primary reason of my hold rating for the SLF stock.

Price Action After The Q4 Report

Earnings season is often a very volatile time in the market, and it seems like everyone’s got their finger on the trigger. But there’s a lot of money to be made during these times. While there are justifiable reasons why investors should sell Sun Life after the latest report, the smart money looks at the future, not the past.

Consider this: on the day the Q4 report was published, SLF was down about 9% from its previous close of $84.63 on Feb 12, 2025.

Remember, the Q4 report was unilaterally taken as bad news. And still, that means some buyers are scooping up shares at a discount. Combined with my assumption that Sun Life’s Q4 issues are temporary, I would rate SLF as a moderate buy opportunity.

Final Verdict on SLF

I've looked at countless financial stocks, and Sun Life Financial remains notable—though it might be because I'm Canadian. Patriotism aside, with Sun Life, you get a steady company that pays reliable dividends and has less relative volatility than its peer group. It’s a company with proven to work through near-term challenges. So, if you're an investor already holding financials, it would make sense to maintain your position while we await more growth signals. If you’re shopping around for financial stocks, consider Sun Life for its discounted price and strong position in the growing Asian markets.

data.title: Wells Fargo: Using A 5.8% Preferred Stock To Bet On Lower Interest Rates
data.description:
Introduction

I don’t think Wells Fargo (NYSE:WFC) needs a lengthy introduction, as pretty much every investor likely is familiar with this large US financial institution. In this article, I will have a closer look at one specific series of preferred shares trading at a discount of almost 20% to its par value, and this paves the way to generate a capital gain if or when the interest rates on the financial markets decrease.

The bank needs a small fraction of its profits to cover the preferred dividends

While the focus of this article will be on the preferred equity, it only makes sense to have a look at how the bank is performing. After all, I’d like to make sure the preferred dividends enjoy an excellent coverage ratio.

In the final quarter of 2024, Wells Fargo announced a net interest income of $11.8B, which represents a QoQ increase of approximately 1%. That of course is a positive evolution, but unfortunately, the bank’s net non-interest expenses increased from $4.4B to approximately $5.3B with a substantial increase in personnel expenses as the main culprit.

This resulted in a pre-tax and pre loan loss provision income of almost $6.5B and after deducting the $1.1B in loan loss provisions and the $120M tax bill, the net profit was approximately $5.26B of which $5.08B was attributable to Wells Fargo. The income statement above also shows the total cost of the preferred dividends was just $278M, which means the bank needed less than 6% of its net profit to cover the preferred dividends. This indicates the preferred dividends are well covered.

That being said, keep in mind the bank definitely benefited from a nice tax benefit in the final quarter of the year. According to the management team, there was a $863M tax benefit related to resolution of other matters, but there were some other non-recurring items as well. The bank recorded $647M in severance expenses while there was a $448M loss on debt securities. So even if we would take the normalized taxes into account, there were some other non-recurring expenses, and on an adjusted basis (removing all non-recurring items), the adjusted net profit would have been (slightly) higher than in the preceding quarter.

A closer look at the Series Z preferred shares

Wells Fargo has several different issues of preferred shares outstanding, but just like a little while ago when I looked at JPMorgan’s preferred shares, I was looking for a low coupon. After all, the current yield of most preferred shares is the same, but as I still think the interest rates on the financial markets will continue to move down, preferred stock with a low fixed preferred dividend yield may provide the potential to generate capital gains as well.

The Series Z preferred shares (NYSE:WFC.PR.Z) currently offer a fixed preferred dividend of $1.1875 per year for a 4.75% yield on the principal amount of $25/unit.

This series of preferred shares is currently trading at $20.47 which means it currently offers a yield of 5.80%. A sub-6% yield definitely isn’t high, but as mentioned before, I like the potential to generate a capital gain if/when/as interest rates go down. This means I don’t consider the Series Z preferred shares to be an income-only idea, but a "total return" idea.

The five-year US Treasury Yield is currently approximately 4.30%, and if this benchmark interest rate would move down to, for instance, 3.25% and the market requires a 200 bps mark-up for preferred equity issued by Wells Fargo, the Series Z preferred shares should be trading at $1.1875/5.25% = $22.61 which is almost exactly 10% higher. If this were to occur within the next two years, the annualized return would be a low single digit number.

Investment thesis

I’d like to emphasize I’m not interested in Wells Fargo’s preferred shares Series Z purely for the income as I’m pursuing a "hybrid" of income and capital gains. I’m aiming for a high single-digit or low double-digit total return in the next two years as lower interest rates on the financial markets should push the Series Z share price higher.

The common shares of Wells Fargo could be interesting as well, but only on market weakness. The company is currently trading at about 14 times its reported earnings for 2024, but as the consensus estimates show, the net income is expected to increase to $6.94/share in 2026, and I’d definitely be willing to pay a price in the $70s for Wells Fargo, given the robust outlook. In situations like this, I usually try to write out of the money put options while waiting for a better entry point. And in case the share price doesn’t decrease, I can just pocket the option premium. A P70 for May 2025, for instance, has an option premium of around $1. Should Wells Fargo trade below $70 at or close to expiry and should the put option get exercised, the true net cost of the position would be around $69.

My main focus is on the preferred shares, but I'm open-minded and will be keeping an eye on the common stock as well.
